exemplar.title:
To what extent does Starbucksâ corporate social responsibility
initiatives and their commitment to sustainability impact
consumer perceptions of the brand?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Defines sustainability accurately and returns to the concept in every major section.
Consistently frames CSR activities using environmental, social and economic pillars.
Analysis of the key concept is woven into multiple sections, linking theory and data back to Starbucks.
Clear, repeated linkage of the concept to organizational practices (wages, C.A.F.E., profitability).
weaknesses: Some passages lapse into extended description (e.g., long quotations of benefits).
Occasional drift with background details not directly tied to sustainability.
Could further contextualize the definition with Starbucksâspecific operational nuances.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although some descriptive passages remain, the work demonstrates an excellent and sustained integration of the key concept throughout. Every major analytical section returns to sustainability, underpinning the investigation and reflecting a comprehensive, tightly woven conceptual framework worthy of the top band.

strengths: Exactly five supporting documents are provided, satisfying the IB requirement.
All sources are directly relevant to CSR, sustainability and financial performance.
Documents are sufficiently in-depth to support the investigationâs core arguments.
weaknesses: Some duplication and lightly used sources limit breadth of perspectives.
Redundant URL entries and inconsistent formatting detract from presentation.
Lacks a wider range of ideas beyond the five core documents.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets the requirement of 3â5 relevant documents that add depth to the analysis, placing this work solidly in the midâband. However, occasional duplication and limited breadth prevent it from reaching the top band for supporting documents.

strengths: Appropriate selection of stakeholder theory, circular business model and profitability ratio.
Methodology statement clearly outlines mixed-methods approach.
Gross profit margin table is well presented and supports quantitative analysis.
weaknesses: Application sometimes remains surface-level (e.g., profitability ratio without industry benchmarks).
Flawed math markup in wage comparison reduces clarity.
Deeper links between selected theories and consumer perceptions could strengthen analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The IA uses an adequate selection of tools and theories applied to Starbucks, fulfilling mid-band expectations. While relevant, the application is uneven in places and lacks the consistent effectiveness required for a higher score.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Effective use of market share and gross profit margin data to link CSR activities with performance.
Insightful connections between farmer productivity improvements and brand perception.
Introduction of greenwashing counterpoints adds depth to evaluation.
Qualitative and quantitative evidence is integrated to address the research question.
weaknesses: Analysis can be overly descriptive, with causal links asserted without primary data.
Missing industry benchmarks (e.g., competitor turnover, cost data) limits evaluative depth.
Some sections lack detailed study parameters (sample size, methods).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates mostly effective reasoning and sustained integration of evidence, moving beyond basic description. Although it falls short of thorough, fully integrated evaluation, it merits the upper mid-band for delivering solid, well-supported analysis with clear relevance to the research question.

strengths: Conclusion explicitly answers the research question âto what extentâ and recognises limitations.
Key findings are coherently summarised and aligned with evidence presented.
Demonstrates a clear, direct link between CSR actions and consumer perceptions/results.
weaknesses: Does not quantify the extent of impact with specific figures.
Lacks weighing of contradictory information or deeper discussion of trade-offs.
Could more critically assess unexpected or negative findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion fully addresses the research question and is consistent with evidence, satisfying the high-band descriptor. Minor weaknesses in quantification and critical nuance prevent a lower or intermediate band, supporting a full-mark award in this criterion.

strengths: Logical organization with clear section headings from introduction through conclusion.
Follows IB IA conventions (title page, table of contents, consistent flow).
weaknesses: Footnote clutter and minor formatting issues affect readability.
Absence of a dedicated methodology subheading reduces navigability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report exhibits an appropriate structure throughout, meeting all expectations for this criterion. Minor formatting suggestions do not detract from its coherent organization.

strengths: All required presentation elements are present (title, word count, contents, citations, appendix).
Citations and supporting documents are properly included, satisfying IB requirements.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting and redundant URL entries.
Inline math and HTML tags appear in the supporting document list.
Some source details (e.g., percentages) lack consistent citation numbering.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission meets the presentation requirements in full. While formatting inconsistencies exist, they do not undermine the presence of required elements, justifying the highest mark for presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows IB structure conventions: title page, table of contents, logical section headings and a coherent flow from introduction to conclusion.  Minor formatting issues (footnote clutter) do not impede readability, so âappropriate structure throughoutâ is achieved.

The Global Impact Report, Macrotrends financial data, academic article on brand loyalty and Starbucks corporate sources all speak directly to CSR, sustainability and financial outcomes, showing clear relevance and adequate depth for an SL IA.  Some duplication and lightly-used sources limit the breadth, so the evidence is ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than fully wide-ranging.
All required elements for an SL IAâtitle, word count, contents, citations and appendix of supporting documentsâare present.  Therefore the strand attains full marks for presentation.
Exactly five supporting documents are listed and labelled, fulfilling the rubric requirement of 3â5 documents.  Therefore the strand merits the band that recognises correct document range.

Each chosen tool is applied to Starbucks and linked to consumer perception, showing adequate relevance.  Nonetheless, applications sometimes remain surface-level (profitability ratio interpreted without comparing to industry benchmarks, circular-model costs asserted without numbers), so effectiveness is uneven, matching the ârelevance not always clear but adequately appliedâ descriptor.

The IA presents basic quantitative data (three-year GPM, U.S. market share) and qualitative evidence from reports; selection is fit for purpose but limited in variety and primary data.  Data are used in calculations and referenced in discussion, satisfying the âadequate selection and useâ band rather than âsufficientâ or âeffectiveâ.

Analysis addresses the research question by linking CSR activities to brand perception and sales, and it notes limitations such as greenwashing and high costs.  However, much reasoning is descriptive and lacks depth (e.g. causal links asserted without survey data), so the work demonstrates âsome analysisâ consistent with the band-3 descriptor.

The student explicitly answers âto what extentâ by asserting that CSR and sustainability have âsignificantlyâ influenced perceptions while recognising some limitations.  The research question is therefore clearly addressed, warranting the top band for this strand.

The student selects three appropriate tools/theories (stakeholder theory, circular business model, profitability ratio).  This constitutes an âadequate selectionâ; additional analytical frameworks (e.g. brand-equity models) could have deepened the study, so the work does not reach the âeffectiveâ standard.

The conclusion restates key findings and is generally in line with the evidence presented, yet it does not weigh contradictory information or quantify impacts.  Thus the consistency with evidence is partial rather than comprehensive, fitting band 2.

Starbucks is at the centre of every section and the student repeatedly links theory and data back to the firm (wages, C.A.F.E. programme, market-share figures, gross-profit margins).  Connections are analysed rather than simply stated, demonstrating how each CSR action affects consumer perceptions.  Integration, however, is not fully sustainedâsome data (e.g. turnover definition) drifts away from the questionâso the work sits comfortably in the âstrong analysis with partial integrationâ level.

The student defines sustainability accurately and returns to the concept in every major section (stakeholder theory, circular model, CSR, profitability).  Discussion consistently frames CSR activities as environmental, social and economic pillars, showing a solid grasp of how the concept underpins the whole investigation.  Analysis is woven into several paragraphs, yet some passages lapse into description (e.g. long quotations of benefits offered), so the integration is strong but not seamless.  This matches the descriptor for a âstrong understanding with partially integrated analysis.â

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly articulated, providing a focused lens for the investigation and guiding the subsequent analysis.
The concept âSustainabilityâ is explicitly stated but could be further contextualized by linking the definition back to Starbucksâ unique operations.
Including the word count meets IB requirements but ensure it updates automatically if the document changes to avoid discrepancies.
The table of contents is comprehensive, but a dedicated methodology subheading could improve navigability for the reader.
The definitions of sustainability and CSR are accurate but heavily descriptive; consider weaving in Starbucksâspecific examples to enhance integration.
The wage comparison uses flawed math markup (\(13.53\frac{8^{12}}{12}\)); simplify to a clear percentage against the industry average.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Selection of the circular business model reflects effective application of sustainability theory to operations.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Section headings effectively organize content, though adding subâsections (e.g., âMethodologyâ) would further refine structure.
Introductory paragraph provides relevant background, but linking historical growth directly to CSR motivations would strengthen coherence.
The methodology statement outlines mixed methods effectively, demonstrating appropriate tool selection early on.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The stakeholder theory section is clear, though a deeper link to how each stakeholder group specifically influences consumer perceptions would enhance analysis.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The C.A.F.E. program is introduced well, but quantifiable outcomes (e.g., percentage increase in farmer income) would bolster the evaluation.
Linking improvements in farmer productivity to brand perception is insightful, demonstrating good analysis of social sustainability effects.
The turnover rate claim lacks industry context; adding competitor benchmarks would clarify if 60% is truly low.
The model description lacks cost data for machinery and processing; including figures would strengthen the costâbenefit analysis.
Conclusion clearly summarizes findings and answers the research question, demonstrating coherence with the evidence provided.
Market share data is used effectively to link CSR actions to business performance, demonstrating adequate quantitative analysis.
Claims about material reductions (e.g., â10 to 20% less plasticâ) need consistent citation numbering and source detail.
Linking environmental CSR to increased loyalty through multiple studies shows good integration but lacks study details (sample size, methods).
The greenwashing discussion introduces critical counterpoints, but referencing real Starbucks controversies would deepen evaluation.
The gross profit margin table is well presented, supporting quantitative analysis and enabling trend identification.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Interpreting GPM trends without industry benchmarks limits insight; compare against sector averages or competitor ratios.
The conclusion would benefit from quantifying âto what extentâ with specific figures (e.g., percentage change in perception metrics).
All five supporting documents are listed, satisfying the IB requirement of 3â5 sources.
Supporting document list formatting is inconsistent (inline math and HTML tags); standardize citation style for clarity.
The reference list includes redundant URL entries; consolidate duplicated sources to improve presentation.
